section.title: Paul's Ministry Among Them
section.sectionRationale: Against critics who questioned his motives, Paul defends the integrity of his ministry in Thessalonica: bold in God, free of flattery and greed, gentle as a nursing mother and firm as a father, working night and day so as not to burden anyone. He then thanks God that they received his message as the word of God, and that they, like the churches in Judea, suffered for it.
section.thesis: True gospel ministry is entrusted by God and answers to God, marked by boldness, purity of motive, tender and fatherly love, and self-giving labor; and the word so preached is received as the very word of God, which then works in believers even through suffering.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the purity of Paul's motives and manner; the tenderness and toil of his fatherly, motherly care; and thanksgiving that they received the word of God and shared in the churches' suffering.

units.label: A ministry of pure motives
units.note: entrusted by God, not pleasing men

verses.text: You yourselves know, brothers, that our visit to you was not in vain.
As you are aware, we had already endured suffering and shameful treatment in Philippi. But in the face of strong opposition, we were bold in our God to speak to you the {{euangelion|gospel}} of God.
For our appeal does not arise from deceit or ulterior motives or trickery.
Instead, we speak as those {{dokimazo|approved}} by God to be entrusted with the gospel, not in order to please men but God, who examines our hearts.
As you know, we never used words of flattery or any pretext for {{pleonexia|greed}}. God is our witness!
Nor did we seek praise from you or from anyone else, although as {{apostolos|apostles}} of Christ we had authority to demand it.

units.label: A ministry of tenderness and toil
units.note: like a nursing mother, like a father

verses.text: On the contrary, we were gentle among you, like a nursing {{trophos|mother}} caring for her children.
We cared so deeply that we were delighted to share with you not only the gospel of God, but our own lives as well. That is how beloved you have become to us.
Surely you recall, brothers, our labor and toil. We worked night and day so that we would not be a burden to anyone while we proclaimed to you the gospel of God.
You are witnesses, and so is God, of how holy, righteous, and blameless our conduct was among you who believed.
For you know that we treated each of you as a father treats his own children,
encouraging you, comforting you, and urging you to walk in a manner {{axios|worthy}} of God, who {{kaleo|calls}} you into His own {{basileia|kingdom}} and glory.

units.label: The word received, the suffering shared
units.note: the word of God at work in believers

verses.text: And we continually thank God because, when you received the word of God that you heard from us, you accepted it not as the word of men, but as it truly is, the {{logos|word}} of God, which is also now at {{energeo|work}} in you who believe.
For you, brothers, became imitators of the churches of God in Judea that are in Christ Jesus. You suffered from your own countrymen the very things they suffered from the Jews,
who killed both the Lord Jesus and their own prophets and drove us out as well. They are displeasing to God and hostile to all men,
hindering us from telling the Gentiles how they may be saved. As a result, they continue to heap up their sins to full capacity; the utmost {{orge|wrath}} has come upon them.

euangelion.gloss: The gospel, the good news of God. Paul repeatedly calls it the gospel of God, stressing that it originates with God and belongs to Him, not to the preacher. He was entrusted with it and answers to God for how he handles it. This governs the whole defense of his ministry.

dokimazo.gloss: To test, examine, and so approve after testing. Paul says he was approved by God, tested and found fit to be entrusted with the gospel, and that God examines his heart. The same word runs beneath the passage: the God who tests the messenger is the one he seeks to please, not men.

pleonexia.gloss: Greed, covetousness, the grasping desire for more. Paul insists he never used ministry as a pretext for greed, a common charge against traveling teachers. His refusal to exploit the Thessalonians for gain is part of proving that his message came from pure, God-approved motives.

apostolos.gloss: An apostle, one sent with the authority of the sender. Paul notes that as apostles of Christ he and his companions could have made demands, yet they laid that right aside. The word underlines both his genuine authority and his deliberate gentleness in not pressing it.

trophos.gloss: A nurse or nursing mother. Paul pictures his gentleness among them as a mother tenderly caring for her own children. It is one of Scripture's most tender images of pastoral care, set beside the father image in verse 11 to capture both nurture and formative guidance.

axios.gloss: Worthily, in a manner that fits or matches. Paul urged them to walk worthy of God, to live in a way that matches the God who called them. It is the goal of his fatherly exhortation: not bare rule-keeping but a life that corresponds to the greatness of the God who saves.

kaleo.gloss: To call or summon. God is the one who calls believers into His own kingdom and glory. The call is effective and gracious, and it defines the Christian's destiny. Paul returns to God as the faithful caller at the letter's close, assuring them the One who calls will also keep them.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, the reign and realm of God. Believers are called into God's own kingdom and glory, their sure destiny in Christ. The word lifts the readers' eyes from present suffering to the coming reign they will share, giving weight to Paul's plea that they walk worthy of it.

logos.gloss: Word, message, or account. Paul thanks God that the Thessalonians received his preaching not as the word of men but as what it truly is, the word of God. The term carries the conviction that in the apostolic gospel God Himself speaks, so the message has divine authority and power.

energeo.gloss: To work, be active, produce an effect. The word of God is now at work in those who believe, energizing and transforming them. The same root gives us energy. Paul's point is that the received word is not inert information but an active, effective power operating within believers.

orge.gloss: Wrath, God's righteous judgment against sin. Paul says the utmost wrath has come upon those who oppose the gospel and hinder the salvation of the Gentiles. It is the sober counterpart to the salvation being offered, and a reminder that persistent opposition to God's saving purpose meets His just judgment.

basic.context: Paul had to leave Thessalonica suddenly, and critics apparently began to slander him, suggesting he was just another traveling con man in it for money and praise. Here he reminds the church what his time among them was actually like: bold, honest, gentle, hardworking, and self-sacrificing. Then he gives thanks that they received his message as God's own word and, like other churches, suffered for it.
basic.hermeneutics: This is partly a defense of Paul's ministry and partly a model of what gospel ministry should look like. We should read it both as Paul answering real accusations and as a lasting pattern: those who handle God's word must do so with pure motives, tenderness, and integrity, aiming to please God rather than people.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul preaches the euangelion, gospel, of God, having been dokimazo, approved and entrusted with it. He rejects pleonexia, greed, and though an apostolos, apostle, with authority, he was gentle as a trophos, nursing mother. He urged them to walk axios, worthy, of the God who kaleo, calls, them into His basileia, kingdom. They received the logos, word, of God, which now energeo, works, in them.
basic.typology: Paul as a suffering messenger stands in the line of the prophets, who were killed by their own people, and points to Christ, the rejected messenger who was killed yet vindicated. The word of God at work in believers echoes God's word that always accomplishes what He sends it to do.
basic.christ: The gospel Paul carries is the good news of Christ, who died and rose. He notes that the Lord Jesus was killed, sharing the fate of the prophets and of His messengers. The kingdom and glory believers are called into are Christ's, and the salvation being offered to the Gentiles is salvation through Him.
basic.god: This is the gospel of God, entrusted by God, aimed at pleasing God, who examines hearts and calls people into His own kingdom and glory. God is the one at work through His word in believers. He is also just: persistent opposition to His saving purpose meets His righteous wrath.
basic.crossReferences: Second Corinthians 4 describes the same refusal to tamper with God's word for gain. Acts 17 recounts the opposition Paul faced in Thessalonica. And Isaiah 55 promises that God's word does not return empty but accomplishes His purpose.
basic.oneStory: God has always sent messengers with His word, and His people have often suffered for receiving it, from the prophets to the Lord Jesus to the young churches. Paul's ministry is one link in that chain: the word of God, faithfully carried and joyfully received, now at work in a suffering church being called into God's kingdom.

context.literary: This section continues the long thanksgiving begun in chapter one, turning to defend and describe the character of Paul's ministry. It moves from negatives, what his ministry was not, to positives, the tenderness of a mother and the exhortation of a father, then to a second thanksgiving for their reception of the word and their sharing in the churches' suffering. Rich family imagery, mother and father, holds the section together.
context.historical: Paul had suffered in Philippi just before coming to Thessalonica, as Acts records, and was then driven out of Thessalonica by a mob after only a few weeks. Traveling teachers and philosophers of the day were often suspected of flattery and greed, so Paul distances himself sharply from that reputation. The suffering the Thessalonians endured came from their own countrymen, paralleling what the Judean churches faced.
context.canonical: This is one of the fullest New Testament portraits of the inner motives and manner of gospel ministry, a companion to passages like 2 Corinthians 2 to 6. Verse 13, on receiving the message as the word of God rather than of men, is a foundational statement of the divine authority of the apostolic word.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To defend the integrity of his ministry against slander and to remind the Thessalonians of the pure, tender, self-giving way the gospel came to them, so they hold fast to it as the word of God amid suffering.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Much of this describes Paul's own conduct, yet it functions as an enduring model for all gospel ministry: purity of motive, gentleness, hard work, and the aim of pleasing God. Verse 13 states a permanent truth, that the apostolic gospel is truly the word of God, not merely human words.

debates.question: How should the strong words about the Jews in verses 14 to 16 be understood?

views.label: A specific charge, not blanket anti-Judaism
views.case: Paul, himself a Jew, indicts particular opponents who killed the prophets and the Lord and were hindering the Gentile mission, not the Jewish people as such; he elsewhere longs and prays for their salvation.

views.label: A prophetic-style lament and warning
views.case: The language echoes the prophets' own rebukes of covenant unfaithfulness; it is a sober warning that opposing God's saving purpose invites His wrath, in the tradition of Israel's own prophets.

debates.bearing: Read rightly, the passage warns against opposing the gospel and never licenses hostility toward Jewish people, whom Paul loved and yearned to see saved.

debates.question: What does it mean that the wrath has come upon them?

views.label: A present and certain judgment
views.case: The perfect-tense wording suggests a judgment already breaking in and settled, the sure fruit of persistent opposition to God's purpose.

views.label: An anticipated final wrath
views.case: The BSB footnote allows at last the wrath has come, pointing to the certainty of God's coming judgment rather than a single past event.

debates.bearing: Either way, the point is sober: to fight against the salvation of others is to store up God's righteous judgment.

study.originalLanguages: Paul calls the message the euangelion of God, the gospel that comes from God, and says he was dokimazo, tested and approved, to be entrusted with it, aiming to please the God who examines hearts. He denies any pleonexia, greed, and notes that though he and his companions were apostolos, apostles, with the right to make demands, they were instead gentle as a trophos, a nursing mother, and formative as a father, urging them to walk axios, worthily, of the God who kaleo, calls, them into His own basileia, kingdom, and glory. The climax is that they received not human words but the logos of God, which now energeo, actively works, in those who believe. The section closes soberly with orge, the wrath resting on those who oppose God's saving purpose.

typology.fulfillment: Paul and the churches share the pattern of the prophets and of the Lord Jesus, who were rejected and killed by their own, yet vindicated by God.

typology.type: The word of God that works
typology.fulfillment: God's living word, which never returns empty, is now at work in believers, fulfilling His purpose as it did throughout Israel's history.

study.christ: The gospel Paul defends is the good news of Christ, entrusted to apostles of Christ. Paul places the Lord Jesus among the prophets killed by their own people, so that the church's suffering conforms to His. The kingdom and glory God calls believers into are the reign of Christ, and the salvation being offered to the Gentiles, which the opponents tried to hinder, is salvation through Him. Christ is thus both the content of the message and the pattern of the messenger's suffering.
study.god: God dominates this passage. It is the gospel of God, entrusted by God, spoken to please God, who tests and examines the heart. God is the one who calls people into His own kingdom and glory, and whose word is now actively at work in believers. He is a God of tender purpose and also of justice: to persist in opposing His saving work and heaping up sin is to meet His righteous wrath. The whole ministry rises and falls on answering to this God rather than to human opinion.

crossReferences.note: Paul refuses to tamper with God's word or use deception, mirroring the purity described here.
The opposition and short stay in Thessalonica that lie behind Paul's defense.
God's word does not return empty but accomplishes His purpose, as it now works in believers.
The killing of the prophets, echoed in Paul's words about opposition to God's messengers.

study.oneStory: God has always sent His word through messengers, and His people have often suffered for receiving it, a pattern running from the prophets through the Lord Jesus to these young churches. Paul's ministry is one faithful link in that chain: the gospel of God carried with pure motives and tender love, joyfully received as God's own word, and now at work in a suffering people being called into God's kingdom and glory. The same word that made Israel and raised the church is still doing God's saving work in the world.

sermon.title: Ministry Like a Nursing Mother: 1 Thessalonians 2:5-8, Part 6
sermon.why: Piper examines how Paul could be gentle as a nursing mother and yet firm as an exhorting father, drawing out the pure motives and self-giving love that mark true gospel ministry in this movement.

quiz.q: What was Paul's aim in his preaching, according to verse 4?

options.text: To please God, who examines hearts, not to please men.
options.note: Verse 4 makes pleasing God, not men, the governing aim.

options.text: To please as many people as possible.
options.note: Paul explicitly denies aiming to please men; he aimed to please God.

options.text: To build a personal following and reputation.
options.note: He refused to seek praise from anyone; his aim was God's approval.

options.text: To make as much money as he could.
options.note: Paul denies any greed and worked night and day to avoid burdening them.

quiz.q: Which two family images does Paul use for his ministry among them?

options.text: A nursing mother caring for her children and a father exhorting his children.
options.note: Verses 7 and 11 give the mother and father images.

options.text: A stern judge and a distant king.
options.note: Paul had authority but chose tenderness, not stern distance.

options.text: A hired servant and a business partner.
options.note: The images are familial and loving, not commercial.

options.text: A soldier and a tax collector.
options.note: Paul uses gentle family imagery, mother and father.

quiz.q: How did the Thessalonians receive Paul's message, and why does it matter?

options.text: Not as the word of men, but as it truly is, the word of God, which works in them.
options.note: Verse 13 states they received it as God's word, now at work in believers.

options.text: As interesting human opinions to consider.
options.note: They received it as God's word, not merely human opinion.

options.text: As one option among many equally valid views.
options.note: They accepted it as the authoritative word of God, not one option among many.

options.text: As a message they could safely ignore.
options.note: They embraced it as God's word, which is actively at work in them.

quiz.q: Why did Paul work night and day while he was among them?

options.text: So as not to be a burden to anyone while he preached the gospel.
options.note: Verse 9 gives this reason for his labor and toil.

options.text: Because he enjoyed the money more than preaching.
options.note: He worked precisely so he would not seek gain from them.

options.text: Because the church forced him to earn his keep.
options.note: He chose this freely, out of love, to avoid burdening them.

options.text: Because he had no interest in the church.
options.note: He labored out of deep care, sharing his very life with them.

quiz.q: Into what does Paul say God calls believers?

options.text: Into His own kingdom and glory.
options.note: Verse 12 names God's kingdom and glory as the believers' calling.

options.text: Into a life of ease and comfort now.
options.note: The calling is to God's kingdom and glory, often reached through suffering.

options.text: Into strict rule-keeping for its own sake.
options.note: He calls them to walk worthy of God, not to bare legalism.

options.text: Into nothing in particular.
options.note: God calls them into a definite and glorious destiny, His kingdom and glory.

questions: Paul aimed to please God, who examines hearts, rather than to please people. Whose approval are you most tempted to live for, and how would seeking God's approval change things?
Paul combined a mother's tenderness with a father's exhortation. In caring for others, do you lean too far toward softness or toward hardness, and where do you need the balance Paul shows?
The Thessalonians received the message as the word of God, not the word of men. How does it change your Bible reading to expect God Himself to speak and to work in you through it?
Paul worked hard so as not to burden others and to keep his motives clear. Where might God be calling you to serve at cost to yourself so your love is unmistakable?
The word of God is at work in those who believe. Where have you seen God's word actually changing you, and where do you long for it to work more deeply?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that some manuscripts and editions attach the clause about apostolic authority to verse 7, reading that the apostles could have been a burden.
The BSB footnote records a variant, we were like young children among you, reflecting a difference of a single letter in the Greek behind gentle and infants.
Where the BSB prints an em dash after his own children, house style renders it as a comma leading into verse 12.
The BSB footnote offers at last the wrath of God has come upon them as an alternative rendering of the closing clause.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Thessalonians 2:1-16. Em dash in verse 11 rendered as a comma per house style.
